name: Booking Embed
description: Calendar-embed contact section that hosts a Cal.com / Calendly / SavvyCal iframe alongside an editorial context column with what-to-expect bullets and an optional email-instead fallback CTA.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column split: editorial copy and a What-to-expect bullet list (with check / clock / video icons) sit on the left; the booking iframe lives in a rounded surface on the right with a min-height so the available slots render without clipping. An optional fallback CTA below the bullets links to email when the visitor prefers it.
descriptions.usage: Use as the primary booking surface for sales calls, intro consults, or content meetings. Drop it into /book, /demo, or /contact pages. Pair with a calm hero and a single closing FAQ — don't stack against another contact section.
descriptions.audienceFit: Founders selling time, B2B SaaS demo flows, consultants with intro calls, conference speakers booking media, and any segment whose first conversion is a 30-minute call rather than a self-serve signup.

name: Contact Chat Embed
description: A chat-first contact section that pairs an editorial reassurance column with a stylised chat-panel preview. The preview animates an agent's first messages and a typing indicator; the primary CTA opens WhatsApp / Intercom / Crisp / Messenger / Telegram / live-chat in a new context.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column split: left side carries the headline, supporting paragraph, and reassurance bullet list (median reply time, real-humans claim, multi-language coverage). Right side renders a rounded chat-panel mock — provider-tagged header with a pulsing online dot, an animated message stream from the named agent, a three-dot typing indicator, and a mock composer footer that turns into the live CTA. Stages messages with a 600ms reveal cadence; respects prefers-reduced-motion.
descriptions.usage: Use as the primary contact section when conversational support is the brand promise — DTC brands with WhatsApp, B2C SaaS with Intercom, agencies with live chat. Drop into /contact, /support, or above the footer on long marketing pages. Avoid on enterprise B2B where a structured form or booking embed reads more credible.
descriptions.audienceFit: Best fit for DTC and consumer brands, B2C SaaS, agencies, and any business whose buyers expect a quick reply from a real person. The named-agent treatment is especially strong for service businesses (clinics, real-estate, coaches) where 'who you'll be talking to' matters as much as the channel itself.

name: Contact Locations with Map
description: A sophisticated contact section featuring an integrated map display alongside location details, designed for professional service firms and corporate businesses with multiple office locations that need to convey trustworthiness and accessibility.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean, structured layout featuring an interactive map paired with organized location cards. Uses generous white space, refined typography, and a balanced grid system. Corporate styling with editorial touches creates a polished, magazine-like presentation with subtle shadows and precise alignment.
descriptions.usage: Essential for businesses with multiple physical locations needing to help customers find nearby offices, stores, or service centers. Combines visual mapping with detailed contact information to reduce friction in the customer journey from discovery to visit.
descriptions.audienceFit: Perfect for established enterprises, professional services firms, retail chains, and B2B companies with physical presence. Appeals to customers who value transparency and accessibility, particularly in finance, healthcare, consulting, and franchise operations.

name: Contact Multi-Step Wizard
description: A wizard-style routing contact form. Step 1 routes the visitor to the right department (sales / support / partnerships / careers); Step 2 collects identity (name, email, optional company / phone); Step 3 captures the message itself. A spring-driven progress bar marks position and AnimatePresence cross-fades between steps, ending in an inline success state.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column section: left column carries the headline plus a vertical step list with checkmark transitions; right column hosts the wizard surface with a slim primary-tinted progress bar at the top. Step 1 renders department options as radio cards with iconified pills and a layoutId checkmark on the active card. Steps 2 and 3 use mono uppercase field labels and the SiteGen CtaButton for the final submit. The success state replaces the wizard with a confirmation panel and a 'send another' link.
descriptions.usage: Use as the primary contact section for any organization that legitimately routes inquiries across departments and wants the visitor to self-route before writing the message — sales, support, press, partnerships, careers. Avoid on small sites where one inbox handles everything; ContactSplitForm or ContactShapesForm are more honest. Pair with a calm hero and a single closing FAQ — don't stack two contact sections.
descriptions.audienceFit: Mid-to-late-stage B2B SaaS, marketplaces, larger agencies, professional services firms, and any organization with multi-department intake. Especially strong for buyers who feel friction filling a generic form and prefer a guided three-step path — the wizard reads as 'we'll respect your time' rather than 'fill out twelve fields'.

name: Contact Form with Shapes
description: A modern contact form enhanced with geometric shapes and editorial styling, designed for corporate businesses seeking to capture leads while maintaining a professional yet approachable brand presence.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean contact form enhanced with geometric shapes and contemporary typography. Features balanced white space, structured input fields, and subtle graphic elements that create visual interest without overwhelming the content. Professional color palette with friendly accent touches maintains approachability.
descriptions.usage: Ideal for business websites needing professional lead capture while displaying location information. Perfect when you want users to easily reach out for consultations, services, or partnerships while maintaining credibility through polished design presentation.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to established B2B companies, consulting firms, and professional service providers targeting corporate clients. Resonates with audiences who value competence and reliability, particularly in finance, law, architecture, or high-end consulting sectors.

name: Split Contact Form
description: Practical split-layout contact section: full form on the left (name, email, optional company and topic select, message), structured info column on the right with phone, email, WhatsApp, address, and a tabular hours table. Higher-density counterpart to ContactShapesForm.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column section with the form left and an elevated info aside right. Form fields are labelled with small uppercase mono labels and pair with the SiteGen CtaButton. Info column lists phone / email / WhatsApp / address rows with iconified pills, plus an optional weekly hours table separated by a hairline rule.
descriptions.usage: Primary contact section for service businesses with a real address and real hours. Drop in /contact pages, on /about as a closing block, or above the footer on long marketing pages. Use ContactBookingEmbed instead when scheduling is the actual goal.
descriptions.audienceFit: Brick-and-mortar service businesses (clinics, agencies, studios), B2B teams that want to publish hours and channels, and any site whose buyers compare 'how do I reach a human' across competitors.

name: Support Tabs Contact
description: Routing-style contact section with category tabs (Sales / Support / Press / Partnerships). Each tab surfaces its own channel list (email, phone, chat, booking link) and an optional per-tab CTA — the visitor self-routes instead of submitting one generic form.

descriptions.descriptive: A vertical pill tab list on the left animates a soft active background between options; the right panel shows the selected tab's description, a divider-separated channel list (email, phone, live chat, calendar) and an optional CTA. Each channel row is clickable when href is present.
descriptions.usage: Use as the contact section for any company that legitimately routes inquiries across departments — sales, support, press, partnerships, careers. Avoid when there's truly only one inbox; use ContactSplitForm or ContactShapesForm instead.
descriptions.audienceFit: Mid-to-late-stage B2B SaaS, marketplaces, larger agencies, and any organization with multi-department intake. Defaults to a calm corporate tone, adapts cleanly to friendlier brands by changing the tab labels and copy.

name: Before/After Carousel
description: Button-navigated carousel of before/after image comparison sliders. One slide is shown at a time; prev/next arrows and pagination dots advance between cases. Each slide is pointer-draggable with keyboard support and labelled before/after pills, useful for restoration, redesign, transformation, and physical-result portfolios.

descriptions.descriptive: Single-slide stage on every viewport; the active slide is a 16:10 surface with the after image full-bleed and the before image clipped from the left by a draggable handle. Keyboard arrows (and Home/End) move the handle in 4% increments. Pagination dots and prev/next chevrons advance between cases with a soft slide+fade transition.
descriptions.usage: Use for any 'we changed this' portfolio — interior renovations, brand refreshes, logo redesigns, before-and-after marketing visuals. Place after a hero or below a stats panel, never alongside another image-heavy carousel on the same page.
descriptions.audienceFit: Architecture and interior design firms, restoration trades, branding agencies, dental and medical aesthetic clinics, photo retouchers, and marketing agencies whose case studies live on transformation imagery.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean two-column layout with balanced visual weight and generous whitespace. Features structured comparison elements with modern typography and subtle dividers. Minimal color palette emphasizes content hierarchy while maintaining professional polish through restrained design choices.
descriptions.usage: Perfect for side-by-side product comparisons, service tier breakdowns, or before/after scenarios. Helps users evaluate options by presenting structured feature lists, pricing plans, or benefit comparisons in an easy-to-scan format that supports decision-making.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to established B2B companies, SaaS platforms, and professional services firms targeting corporate buyers. Resonates with audiences who value transparency and detailed evaluation processes, particularly in finance, technology, and consulting sectors.

name: Editorial Framed Split
description: A refined split layout featuring framed content sections that creates editorial-style hierarchy, perfect for professional services and established brands showcasing their story or key features with understated elegance.

descriptions.descriptive: A sophisticated two-column layout featuring generous whitespace, refined typography, and subtle framing elements. Clean lines and restrained visual hierarchy create an editorial magazine aesthetic with classic proportions and minimal ornamentation.
descriptions.usage: Perfect for presenting detailed feature explanations, company story narratives, or service descriptions where content depth matters. Balances substantial text blocks with supporting visuals to guide readers through complex information.
descriptions.audienceFit: Ideal for established professional services, consulting firms, luxury brands, or publications targeting educated audiences who value substance over flash. Appeals to clients seeking expertise and long-term partnerships.

name: Case Study Gallery
description: Long-form case study with hero image, project meta strip, alternating zig-zag chapters (problem / approach / result), and an organic-numbers metric band. Generous editorial spacing throughout.

descriptions.descriptive: Hero image at 16:9 anchors a wide centered column. Below, an underlined meta strip lists client / role / year / team in mono caps. Three chapters alternate image-left then image-right at lg breakpoint and stack on mobile. A dark metric band lifts the outcomes in mono numerals — designed for organic numbers (47.2 percent, 3,847) rather than rounded vanity stats. Closing CTA is left-aligned to keep the editorial rhythm.
descriptions.usage: Use as a standalone case study page or as the centerpiece of a portfolio detail route. Place after a hero brand-statement and before related work or a closing CTA. Avoid pairing with a masonry or portfolio-strip gallery on the same page — those serve the index view, not the deep dive.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B agencies showing client work, design studios with longer narratives, consultancies with quantified outcomes, internal-tool teams documenting transformation projects. Suits readers who want depth, not a gallery wall.

name: Image-Text Editorial Gallery
description: Magazine-style alternating image-and-caption spread. Each entry zig-zags the image side and applies a small vertical offset so the column drifts like an editorial layout. Serif headlines pair with sans-serif body.

descriptions.descriptive: A 12-column grid where the image takes 7 columns and the copy takes 5, alternating sides per entry. The image column carries a small translate-y offset (positive on reversed rows, negative on default rows) so the page drifts vertically and avoids the generic flush-row rhythm. A 1px hairline rule sits above each entry label, evoking magazine separators. Headings use the serif stack; body and labels stay in sans for contrast.
descriptions.usage: Use as the deep-narrative section on portfolio sites, brand storytelling pages, magazine archives, hospitality experience pages, and craft-led commerce. Place after a brand-statement hero. Avoid stacking with another zigzag layout (ZigzagAlternatingSplit) — they read as the same beat repeated.
descriptions.audienceFit: Editorial brands, hotels and restaurants, fashion houses with story-led marketing, longform-friendly portfolios, conferences with documented programming. Suits visitors who came to read, not to scan.

name: Dual-Offer Split CTA
description: Two side-by-side CTA cards offering the visitor a clear choice — typically self-serve vs. assisted, free vs. enterprise, or audience A vs. audience B. Optional primary-led emphasis tints the first card on bg-primary.

descriptions.descriptive: Mirrored card layout with eyebrow, title, description, optional bullet list, CTA, and footnote per side. Two emphasis modes: balanced (both cards on bg-base-100 with a hairline ring) or primary-led (left card on bg-primary, right card neutral). Cards stagger in on view at 80ms intervals.
descriptions.usage: Use when the page must offer two genuinely different paths and either is a valid choice — most often self-serve vs. talk-to-sales, but also founder-vs-agency, free-vs-enterprise, or hire-vs-license. Place near the bottom of long marketing pages.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS routing buyers to the right path, agency sites separating audiences, marketplaces with two-sided users, and any segmented-audience site whose buyers self-identify clearly.

name: Hover Reveal List CTA
description: An editorial stacked-list CTA where each row is a category or product line whose image becomes the row's background on hover. Designed for businesses with multiple product divisions (bakeries, ateliers, studios, restaurants with menu sections) where each row is itself a click target into a sub-page.

descriptions.descriptive: A magazine-style index laid out as full-width hoverable rows. Each row pairs a counter, a large display label, and a subtle arrow; on hover the row's image fades in as the row's own background — a slow zoom plus a left-weighted scrim — and surrounding rows dim to put the focal row forward. Spring physics give the label a tactile slide and the arrow a quick rotate-into-place.
descriptions.usage: Deploy when you need to present multiple product lines, service categories, or portfolio sections without committing to a feature grid. Especially strong for bakeries (breads, cakes, pies, savory), ateliers (collections), restaurants (menu sections), and studios (disciplines). The list-as-CTA pattern keeps the page calm while making every row a discoverable doorway, with the row-fill image reveal carrying the visual interest.
descriptions.audienceFit: Works for premium and editorial brands — artisan food, fashion, hospitality, design studios — where the catalog is part of the brand voice. The interaction reads as confident and considered rather than salesy.

name: CTA Image Backdrop
description: A sophisticated call-to-action that overlays text on a full-width background image, creating an editorial magazine-like aesthetic perfect for luxury brands and premium service providers wanting to combine compelling visuals with clear action prompts.

descriptions.descriptive: A sophisticated call-to-action featuring a large, high-quality background image with refined typography overlays. Clean lines and generous whitespace create breathing room, while the editorial styling emphasizes visual storytelling through carefully curated photography and minimal design elements that exude calm luxury.
descriptions.usage: Deploy when you need to combine brand narrative with conversion action, particularly for premium offerings or story-driven campaigns. Perfect for landing pages where emotional connection precedes the ask, allowing brands to establish credibility and context before presenting their primary call-to-action.
descriptions.audienceFit: Resonates with established premium brands, creative agencies, luxury services, and lifestyle companies targeting discerning customers who value quality over quantity. Appeals to audiences that appreciate sophisticated design and expect brands to demonstrate substance before making conversion requests.

name: CTA Inline Calculator
description: An interactive estimate widget paired with a primary CTA — the visitor adjusts sliders for inputs like loan amount or seat count and sees the computed result in tabular font-mono. Inputs ride along to the destination URL as querystring params for form pre-fill.

descriptions.descriptive: Asymmetric two-column layout: a copy column on the left with eyebrow, headline, description, and labelled range sliders that update instantly with min/max ticks rendered as small mono labels; a result panel on the right with a primary numeric output rendered as the hero numeral, optional secondary outputs in a 2-column grid, and a primary CTA whose href carries the slider values as querystring parameters for downstream pre-fill.
descriptions.usage: Use when the conversion benefits from the visitor seeing a tangible number against their own inputs before the form ask — mortgage payments, savings projections, plan totals, freight estimates. Keep it to 1–4 inputs and a single hero output. The footnote is the right place for assumptions that would otherwise clutter the result panel.
descriptions.audienceFit: Strong fit for fintech, insurance, B2B SaaS pricing pages, freight, real estate, and any segment whose customers expect to see a number before they share contact details. Consumer brands without quantified benefits should reach for CtaTestimonialPaired or CtaFormInline instead. The `outputs[].compute` slot is a function and is not AI-fillable — leave it as a developer-supplied slot.

name: Sticky Image List CTA
description: An asymmetric split CTA where a single image stays sticky on one side of the viewport while the visitor scrolls through a stack of CTA items on the other. Each item carries its own image, and the sticky photograph cross-fades to match whichever item is currently being read — so the visual stays anchored even as the page reaches the bottom CTAs.

descriptions.descriptive: A two-column editorial split with the image column pinned via position:sticky. As the reader scrolls past each CTA item on the right, an IntersectionObserver tracks which item sits closest to the viewport center, and the sticky image cross-fades to that item's photograph with a soft scale-out exit. A small numeric pill on the image (01 / 06) ties the picture to its row, and per-item inline arrows let each row act as its own click target before the section's footer CTA closes the block.
descriptions.usage: Deploy when you have three to six related CTAs that share a common visual frame — service tiers, portfolio chapters, restaurant menu sections, atelier collections — and you want the image presence to carry through the entire scroll instead of disappearing as the visitor reads down. The sticky image keeps the brand's photographic mood in view all the way to the conversion CTA at the bottom, which makes it especially strong as a closing section before the footer.
descriptions.audienceFit: Premium and editorial brands that lead with imagery — hospitality, fashion, food, design studios, real-estate developers. The pacing reads as confident and considered: the image holds, the copy moves, the visitor never loses the picture they liked.

name: CTA Testimonial Paired
description: A primary call-to-action paired with an embedded customer quote and optional KPI, so the conversion ask sits next to a fragment of social proof that answers 'why should I trust this' in the same eye-fixation.

descriptions.descriptive: Asymmetric two-column layout: a calm copy column on the left with eyebrow, headline, supporting paragraph, primary CTA, optional secondary link, and check-bullet perks; a quote card on the right with an oversized open-quote glyph, optional KPI numeral in font-mono, the testimonial body, and a portrait + attribution footer. Three tones (neutral, muted, inverse) and a stacked layout for narrower contexts.
descriptions.usage: Use as the closing CTA on landing pages, pricing pages, and case-study indexes where the primary friction is trust rather than awareness. Pair the metric numeral with a quantifiable, organic outcome ('47.2% lift', '9 weeks to ROI') rather than a round vanity number.
descriptions.audienceFit: Fits B2B SaaS, professional services, agencies, and any segment whose customers buy on credibility. The inverse tone leans into high-attention launches; muted tone is the safer default for content-led pages.

name: CTA Video Backdrop
description: A high-impact CTA section with a looping muted background video, layered overlay, and a calm copy column with primary and secondary actions. Pauses on prefers-reduced-motion and surfaces play/mute controls so visitors can opt into or out of the motion.

descriptions.descriptive: A full-bleed section with a muted, autoplaying background video and a darkening overlay. A left-aligned copy column carries an eyebrow, oversized headline, supporting paragraph, and primary + secondary CTAs. Bottom-right pill houses a play/pause and mute toggle that floats above the video without blocking the headline.
descriptions.usage: Reach for it once per page at most — typically the closing conversion moment on a product launch or brand-led landing page. Pair with a 5–8 second loop encoded under 3 MB; static poster handles slow connections and reduced-motion preferences gracefully.
descriptions.audienceFit: Best for product launches, premium consumer goods, hospitality, and creative agencies whose differentiation hinges on motion and atmosphere. Avoid on dense B2B SaaS pages where motion competes with feature comprehension.

name: FAQ Categorized
description: An FAQ section with a vertical category rail and a paired question accordion, ideal for help centers and policy hubs that group naturally into topic buckets. The sidebar collapses to a horizontal scroll strip below md, and category switching cross-fades the question list with a spring-driven active-state marker.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column editorial layout: a slim left rail with category labels and live question counts, paired with a divider-line accordion of the active category's questions. Active-state is communicated by a spring-physics primary marker rail and a tinted question count chip, with a cross-fade transition between categories.
descriptions.usage: Use when an FAQ library exceeds twelve questions and groups cleanly into three to six distinct topic areas — Billing, Security, Implementation, etc. The sidebar acts as a content map, helping visitors locate the right bucket before they read individual questions.
descriptions.audienceFit: Strong fit for B2B SaaS, fintech, and regulated industries where customers ask category-shaped questions (legal, security, compliance, billing) and value being able to mentally pre-filter before reading. Works equally well on dedicated help-center pages and as the bottom-of-funnel section on long pricing or product pages.

name: FAQ Solutions
description: A bold, editorial-style FAQ section with modern typography and dynamic layouts that builds trust through clear, energetic presentation of common questions and answers. Perfect for tech companies, agencies, and professional services that want to showcase expertise while maintaining an approachable, confident brand voice.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean, structured FAQ layout with bold typography and generous white space. Features editorial-style headings with strong contrast and modern geometric elements. The design emphasizes readability through clear hierarchy and professional spacing, creating an energetic yet organized visual rhythm.
descriptions.usage: Deploy when customers need quick answers to common questions without navigating away from key pages. Ideal for reducing support tickets and building confidence during decision-making moments. Works best on product pages, pricing sections, or dedicated help areas.
descriptions.audienceFit: Perfect for B2B software companies, consulting firms, and professional service providers targeting decision-makers who value efficiency and transparency. Appeals to businesses that want to project expertise while maintaining approachable customer communication.

name: Tabbed FAQ
description: A tabbed FAQ that groups questions into named categories (Billing, Security, Implementation) with an animated underline tab strip and a cross-fading accordion panel per tab.

descriptions.descriptive: Horizontal tab strip with an animated underline marks the active category; selecting a tab cross-fades the underlying accordion. Each tab carries an optional one-line description and its own short list of question/answer pairs.
descriptions.usage: Use when total question count exceeds roughly ten and the questions split cleanly into segments — pricing, privacy, implementation, support. Pair after a pricing tier section or before a closing CTA strip.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS, fintech, and infrastructure companies whose buyers ask layered questions across distinct concern areas. Defaults to a calm, corporate tone but adapts cleanly to editorial or services briefs.

name: Columns & Social Footer
description: A medium-density utility footer with a brand block, up to four sitemap columns, and a dedicated social column rendering each network as a handle-style card. No newsletter form — pair above with a closing CTA section. Sits on bg-base-200 for quieter visual weight than FooterMega.

descriptions.descriptive: Top section is a five-column grid: a brand block (logo, tagline, optional region pill), three sitemap columns, and a social column where each network renders as a small handle card (icon + display handle + arrow chevron). Bottom row carries copyright and optional inline legal links. Renders on bg-base-200 with bg-base-100 surfaces for the social handle cards — noticeably quieter than FooterMega's bg-neutral treatment.
descriptions.usage: Use as the site-wide footer for product, marketplace, or content sites that want strong social discoverability without a newsletter capture row. The page must already carry a closing CTA above this footer (CtaMinimalStrip, CtaEditorialSplit). Don't stack with another footer — pick exactly one footer per generated site.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2C product brands, marketplaces, creator-led brands, and SMBs whose social presence (Instagram, TikTok, WhatsApp) is the primary follow-on channel and deserves more than a row of generic icons.

name: Mega Footer
description: A high-density 'mega-footer' with a newsletter capture row, a 4-5 column sitemap, a copyright line, optional legal links and a social row. Sits on the neutral surface tokens and is the canonical site-bottom for content-heavy product pages.

descriptions.descriptive: Three stacked sections: a newsletter section with eyebrow / headline / description on the left and an inline form (or a check-icon success state) on the right; a sitemap with the brand block plus up to four navigation columns, each link supporting an optional 'New' / count badge; and a bottom bar with copyright, optional legal links, and a row of social icons. Renders on bg-neutral with hairline neutral-content/10 dividers between sections.
descriptions.usage: Use as the site-wide footer when the page already carries a closing CTA (CtaMinimalStrip, CtaCountdown). Don't stack with FooterPulse or FooterReveal — pick exactly one footer per generated site.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS, fintech, marketplaces, and content-heavy publishers whose footers carry real navigation work — sitemap, trust links, and an active newsletter list.

name: Pulse Editorial Footer
description: A clean, magazine-style footer with refined typography and minimal visual elements that builds credibility for editorial websites, publishing platforms, and content-focused businesses requiring professional authority.

descriptions.descriptive: A streamlined footer with generous whitespace and typography-forward design. Features clean sans-serif text hierarchy, subtle dividing lines, and restrained color palette. The editorial influence shows in careful text spacing and journalistic information architecture that feels both sophisticated and uncluttered.
descriptions.usage: Anchors content-heavy websites where credibility and readability matter most. Perfect for publications, blogs, news sites, and professional services that need to maintain reader trust while providing essential navigation and contact information without visual distraction from the main content.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to established media companies, professional publications, consulting firms, and knowledge-based businesses. Resonates with educated audiences who value substance over flashiness, making it ideal for brands positioning themselves as thought leaders or authoritative information sources.

name: Sticky Reveal Footer
description: A modern footer that elegantly reveals itself as users scroll, featuring clean typography and minimal design elements. Perfect for editorial sites, professional services, and brands seeking a sophisticated, trustworthy presence.

descriptions.descriptive: A sleek footer that emerges smoothly as users scroll, featuring clean typography and generous white space. The minimal design uses subtle shadows and refined spacing to create an editorial magazine aesthetic with polished, understated elegance.
descriptions.usage: Serves as the primary site footer while maintaining visual hierarchy through progressive disclosure. Reveals contact information, legal links, and brand elements without overwhelming the main content experience until users naturally reach the page bottom.
descriptions.audienceFit: Perfect for professional services firms, design agencies, consulting practices, and B2B companies that value sophisticated brand presentation. Appeals to discerning clients who associate clean design with competence and reliability.

name: Thumbnail Nav Carousel
description: An e-commerce style image viewer with a primary stage and a thumbnail strip beneath. Visitors can drag, click thumbnails, or use keyboard arrows to advance — ideal for product detail pages, portfolio cases, and gallery walks where the same item is shown from multiple angles.

descriptions.descriptive: A primary image stage on top with a horizontally-scrollable thumbnail strip below. Active thumbnails get a primary-colored ring and a shared-layout indicator that animates between selections. The main stage uses a directional spring transition, supports drag-to-advance, and exposes prev/next buttons over the image. A small monospace counter pill in the corner reinforces position.
descriptions.usage: Use to display the same subject from multiple angles, contexts, or stages — product detail pages, real-estate listings, portfolio case studies, before-and-after stages of a transformation. Avoid pairing with another image-heavy carousel on the same page.
descriptions.audienceFit: E-commerce stores, real-estate agencies, photographers, design portfolios, and any service business whose value is communicated through detailed visuals from multiple perspectives.

name: Lightbox Grid Gallery
description: Uniform aspect-square thumbnail grid with a keyboard-driven lightbox modal. Click opens; Escape closes; Arrow keys navigate. Body scroll locks during overlay.

descriptions.descriptive: A clean square-aspect grid (2, 3, or 4 columns) where each tile is a button — no decorative chrome, just a hover scale and a focusable zoom-in glyph. Click opens a 5xl-wide lightbox with backdrop-blur, prev/next chevrons, caption + meta, and a focus-trapped close. Keyboard navigation (Esc, ←, →) is wired into a single window listener that mounts only while the modal is open.
descriptions.usage: Use on product photo galleries, conference recap pages, event lookbooks, real-estate listings, and any segment where the click-to-zoom interaction is part of the buyer's evaluation. Place after a hero or testimonial section. Avoid pairing with another gallery in the same flow — visitors only need one viewing pattern.
descriptions.audienceFit: E-commerce with high product photography standards, real-estate listings, conference and venue marketing, photographer client galleries, archival showcase pages.

name: Masonry Gallery
description: Pinterest-style asymmetric multi-column gallery built on CSS columns. Each tile picks a shape (tall, wide, square) and reveals title plus category on hover or focus.

descriptions.descriptive: True masonry via CSS `columns` so tiles flow into the shortest column without layout JavaScript. Each tile carries a shape bias (`tall`, `wide`, default square), giving the wall an organic rhythm. A bottom-anchored gradient surfaces title and category on hover and on keyboard focus, so meta stays discoverable without cluttering the static state.
descriptions.usage: Use as the work-wall on portfolio sites, editorial archives, lookbooks, and product photography indexes. Place after a brand-statement hero and before a closing CTA. Avoid pairing with another rigid grid layout (CardGrid, SimpleGrid) on the same page — the visual rhythm cancels out.
descriptions.audienceFit: Photographers, illustrators, design studios, fashion and lifestyle brands, hospitality groups — any segment where image-density itself is the pitch.

name: Full-Bleed Slider Carousel
description: An edge-to-edge image rotator with overlaid eyebrow, headline, description, and CTA per slide. Auto-advances with a progress-bar dot indicator and pauses on hover or focus. Designed to anchor a page as a hero replacement when the brand story spans multiple imagery beats.

descriptions.descriptive: Full-bleed background image with a subtle neutral-toned gradient for caption legibility. Each slide carries its own eyebrow / headline / description / CTA; transitions are spring-animated with a soft scale-on-enter. Pagination dots double as a linear progress bar for the active slide; hover, focus, and an explicit pause button all halt the auto-advance.
descriptions.usage: Anchors a page as a hero replacement when there are multiple campaigns, projects, or stories to lead with. Works well for travel, hospitality, editorial, and product launch sites. Avoid stacking with a regular hero or another image-heavy carousel on the same page.
descriptions.audienceFit: Travel and hospitality brands, editorial and journalism sites, premium e-commerce launching seasonal collections, agencies leading with case work, and any brand whose story is carried by full-frame imagery.

name: Asymmetric Stack Hero
description: An oversized typographic hero with a three-row stacked headline, intentional row offsets, and two peeking rotated image cards. Built for editorial, fashion, and culture-led brands that want the type itself to be the visual.

descriptions.descriptive: Three large serif rows step diagonally — left-aligned, right-aligned, then indented again — with an italic accent on the middle row and an optional colored accent word. Two small image cards peek out from opposite quadrants on a slight rotation, breaking the symmetry without competing with the type.
descriptions.usage: Best as a homepage opener for editorial brands, fashion houses, cultural institutions, music labels, and culture-led products where typography is the primary asset. Use peek images to suggest scope or season without committing to a hero photograph.
descriptions.audienceFit: Reads as confident and current to design-aware audiences. Especially effective for brands targeting buyers who associate restraint, scale, and italic counterpoints with editorial credibility.

name: Bakery Editorial Hero
description: An editorial hero with a framed card layout, hand-script accent word, circular stamp seal, and a hero product photo that breaks out of the right column. Designed for artisan food, bakery, deli, café, and small-batch consumer brands that want a tactile, story-led first impression.

descriptions.descriptive: A boxed editorial hero with a soft grid background, italicized hand-script accent inside a serif headline, a circular dashed stamp seal, and floating decorative seed glyphs that gently drift in place. The product image deliberately breaks out of the right column, giving the layout an asymmetric, magazine-style focal weight.
descriptions.usage: Opens artisan and small-batch consumer sites where the headline is half-craft / half-product. Pairs the editorial mood of a recipe column with a clear primary CTA, so it works equally well for storytelling brands and direct-to-consumer storefronts.
descriptions.audienceFit: Bakeries, delis, cafés, butchers, chocolatiers, and other food-and-craft brands targeting taste-driven local audiences. Also fits curated retail, perfumery, and slow-living lifestyle brands that want warmth without losing structure.

name: Device Chrome Hero
description: A SaaS-classic hero pairing a tight content column with a product screenshot mounted in the device frame of your choice — browser window, macbook lid, phone, or tablet. Built for software products whose conversion lever is showing the actual UI.

descriptions.descriptive: A 5/7 split with the headline column on the left (eyebrow status pill, optional highlighted word in the headline, subheadline, primary + secondary CTAs, optional trust badges) and the product screenshot on the right wrapped in a chosen device chrome. Browser frame includes a fake address bar; macbook frame mimics a laptop lid; phone and tablet frames render rounded device shells. A soft primary-color glow sits behind the device for depth.
descriptions.usage: Use as the homepage opener for SaaS, mobile apps, productivity tools, and any product whose interface is its strongest argument. Choose the device frame that matches where the user lives: browser for B2B web apps, phone for consumer mobile, macbook for design tools, tablet for enterprise field tools.
descriptions.audienceFit: Reads as credible to founders, product leaders, and buyers who have seen this pattern done well by Linear, Vercel, and Notion. Avoid for brands selling experience-led products (hospitality, fashion, restaurants) where a screenshot sells nothing.

name: Grid Gallery Hero
description: A bento-style hero that pairs an editorial content column with an asymmetric four-tile gallery, ideal for portfolios, hospitality brands, and lifestyle products that lead with imagery.

descriptions.descriptive: A 12-column split places the headline at lower-left and a four-tile bento gallery at right, with one tall feature image and three supporting cells in varied proportions. Captions float over photo corners on small chips, and tiles scale subtly on hover.
descriptions.usage: Use as the homepage opener for studios, design agencies, hotels, restaurants, or boutique commerce sites where the work itself sells the brand. Pairs well with a stats strip below to anchor credibility before the user scrolls into project details.
descriptions.audienceFit: Speaks to design-aware visitors who expect a portfolio-grade first impression — creative directors, hospitality managers, gallerists, and editorial commerce buyers.

name: Newsroom Ticker Hero
description: An editorial-newsroom hero with a live data ticker, oversized serif headline, lede paragraph, and a side rail of featured stories. Built for publications, financial-data products, research firms, and any brand that wants to read like a front page.

descriptions.descriptive: A monospace ticker scrolls along the top edge with up/down sentiment glyphs and trend tints. Below, an issue rule frames a serif display headline and a horizontal divider tucks the lede beneath the title. The right rail stacks featured stories with kickers, optional thumbnails, and meta lines that read like a print contents page.
descriptions.usage: Best for media properties, research firms, fintech and B2B SaaS data products, and any homepage where credibility comes from showing live information at the top. The side rail can be repurposed for case studies, latest releases, or featured projects.
descriptions.audienceFit: Reads as authoritative to professional readers — investors, analysts, journalists, policy folk, and B2B buyers — who associate density and serif typography with trustworthy reporting.

name: Polaroid Collage Hero
description: A playful, scrapbook-style hero with three to four rotated polaroid cards in an asymmetric collage, paired with an italic script accent in the headline. Built for cafes, weddings, family-run shops, lifestyle blogs, and brands that lead with warmth.

descriptions.descriptive: Polaroid frames sit on the right with hand-placed rotation, soft shadows, and a small tape strip at the top of each. Hovering nudges them upright. Captions read in italic serif. The headline pairs a confident sans with a script-style accent word, and the section sits on a warm muted base.
descriptions.usage: Best for hospitality, wedding photographers, family-run cafes, slow-living brands, lifestyle publications, and any homepage where intimacy and warmth are the primary feeling. On mobile, the collage flattens into a 2x2 grid that keeps the rotation but loses the depth.
descriptions.audienceFit: Reads as warm and personal to consumer audiences who value handcraft and story over polish. Best avoided for technical, financial, or enterprise contexts where the playfulness can read as unserious.

name: Split Form Hero
description: A conversion-led hero that pairs a left-aligned message with an inline lead-capture form on the right. Designed for SaaS landing pages, financial services, B2B sign-up flows, and any campaign measured by inbound form fills.

descriptions.descriptive: An eyebrow, balanced display headline, supporting paragraph, and a small set of trust bullets occupy the left column. The right column hosts a card-style form with optional name, email, and phone fields, a primary submit button, and a fine-print disclaimer. After submit, the card replaces itself with an inline confirmation state.
descriptions.usage: Best for SaaS waitlists, sales-led B2B landing pages, financial services lead capture, course sign-ups, and any homepage where the primary metric is inbound form fills. Compose with a stats strip and FAQ section below to handle objections without requiring a scroll.
descriptions.audienceFit: Reads as direct and credible to commercial buyers and decision-makers who expect form-driven inquiry as the standard pattern. Trust bullets help mid-market and enterprise audiences self-qualify before submitting.

name: Split Image Hero
description: A split-screen hero section that pairs impactful imagery with headline content, designed for professional service firms and corporate brands seeking an elegant, editorial-style first impression.

descriptions.descriptive: A sophisticated split-screen layout featuring a prominent image occupying half the viewport alongside clean typography and minimal content. The design emphasizes white space, refined typography hierarchy, and balanced proportions that convey editorial sophistication with corporate polish.
descriptions.usage: Perfect for homepage hero sections where visual storytelling needs equal weight with messaging. Ideal when you have compelling photography or graphics that deserve prominence while maintaining space for clear value propositions and primary calls-to-action.
descriptions.audienceFit: Professional services firms, B2B companies, consulting agencies, and established brands seeking to project authority and credibility. Appeals to discerning audiences who value quality presentation and expect polished, magazine-quality digital experiences.

name: Video Backdrop Hero
description: A full-bleed cinematic hero with optional looping video backdrop, tinted overlay, and an asymmetric content block anchored to the bottom edge. Built for hospitality, fashion campaigns, agencies, and any brand whose product is a felt experience.

descriptions.descriptive: An ambient video plays muted and looping over a poster fallback, layered with a configurable neutral tint and a soft bottom-up gradient for legibility. Headline, subheadline, and CTAs anchor in a chosen quadrant, with an optional metadata strip along the bottom for credits, locations, or release dates.
descriptions.usage: Use as the homepage opener for brands that have strong moving footage — hotels, fashion campaigns, agencies, restaurants, festivals, and media properties. Falls back to the poster image when no video URL is provided or when the user prefers reduced motion.
descriptions.audienceFit: Works for design-aware visitors who associate cinematic motion with quality. Best for brands that already have professional video assets and need a confident first frame.

name: Magnify Dock Navbar
description: A floating bottom-anchored dock with cursor-driven icon magnification, paired with a quiet top bar for the logo and CTA. Built for portfolio sites and creative studios that want navigation to feel tactile and modern without dominating the page.

descriptions.descriptive: A macOS-style dock pinned to the bottom centre of the viewport. Each icon scales smoothly toward the cursor through spring physics, with a tooltip label appearing above the hovered tile. The top of the page is reserved for a minimal logo and a single optional CTA, keeping the hero composition clear.
descriptions.usage: Best for portfolio sites, creative agencies, and product landing pages where the navigation can feel like a controlled flourish rather than a utility bar. Pair with image-led heroes and editorial layouts where horizontal top-bar real estate is at a premium.
descriptions.audienceFit: Resonates with design-forward audiences and product-curious visitors who recognise the dock metaphor instantly. Less appropriate for utility-heavy SaaS or compliance-driven enterprise sites where conventional top navigation reduces friction.

name: Mega Panel Navbar
description: A wide flat top bar that opens a structured multi-column mega panel on hover, ideal for SaaS products and enterprise sites with deep information architecture that need to surface featured content alongside grouped link lists.

descriptions.descriptive: A clean horizontal top bar that reveals a full-width mega panel on hover, organising links into titled columns with optional descriptions, badges, and a featured card on the right. The panel sits on a soft backdrop-blur surface with subtle borders and disciplined typography hierarchy.
descriptions.usage: Best for sites with multiple product surfaces, solution categories, or large resource libraries that need to expose deep navigation without overwhelming the top bar. The featured card slot is ideal for pinning launches, case studies, or signature articles in context.
descriptions.audienceFit: Fits B2B SaaS, enterprise platforms, fintech, and developer tooling brands selling to evaluators who scan for breadth before depth. The dense panel rewards skim-readers without sacrificing the calm of a minimal collapsed state.

name: Pill Glass Navbar
description: A sleek navigation bar with pill-shaped glass elements that creates a refined, editorial aesthetic perfect for professional service firms, design agencies, and premium content publishers seeking an understated yet sophisticated header.

descriptions.descriptive: A sleek navigation bar featuring rounded pill-shaped buttons with subtle glass-like transparency effects. Clean typography and generous whitespace create an uncluttered, sophisticated appearance with soft edges and refined visual hierarchy that feels contemporary and polished.
descriptions.usage: Perfect for primary site navigation where users need clear, intuitive access to main sections. The prominent yet elegant design guides visitors smoothly between pages while maintaining focus on content, ideal for homepages and landing pages requiring seamless user flow.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to established businesses, creative agencies, and professional service firms targeting discerning clients. The refined aesthetic resonates with brands emphasizing quality and expertise, particularly in consulting, design, finance, or premium B2B services where credibility matters most.

name: Sticky Navbar
description: A fixed navigation bar that remains visible while scrolling, featuring clean typography and subtle styling perfect for corporate websites and professional services that need consistent access to key menu items.

descriptions.descriptive: A streamlined horizontal navigation bar that remains fixed at the top of the viewport while scrolling. Features clean typography, generous white space, and subtle shadow effects. The minimal design uses corporate color schemes with well-spaced menu items and refined hover states that maintain visual hierarchy.
descriptions.usage: Essential for websites requiring constant access to main navigation regardless of scroll position. Perfect for long-form content sites, multi-page corporate websites, and service platforms where users need to navigate between sections without returning to the top of the page.
descriptions.audienceFit: Ideal for established B2B companies, professional service firms, and corporate enterprises seeking to project reliability and competence. Appeals to business audiences who value efficiency and expect polished, convention-following interfaces that facilitate quick decision-making.

name: Pricing Freemium Ladder
description: A staircase visualization where each tier sits one rung higher than the previous, connected by a dashed climb line. Reads as a journey instead of three brochure cards — useful for products with a true freemium funnel where the buyer needs to see the climb.

descriptions.descriptive: Cards positioned absolutely on a fixed-height stage so each step rises 80px above the previous one and shifts ~17% to the right. A dashed diagonal SVG line traces the climb. The recommended step inverts to a dark surface and lifts into a higher z-index, with a small primary 'Start here' pill that anchors the eye.
descriptions.usage: Use on freemium SaaS, creator tools, or developer products where the value of the funnel is the climb itself. Lands well between problem framing and FAQ; pairs poorly with the brochure-card pricing variants — pick one mental model per page.
descriptions.audienceFit: Self-serve buyers, indie hackers, and developer-tooling evaluators who appreciate seeing the full progression at once. Suits playful and bold brands; less suited to procurement-led B2B where a flat comparison table reads faster.

name: Pricing Monthly Annual Toggle
description: Tier cards with an animated monthly / annual billing-cycle switch. The toggle uses a shared-layoutId sliding pill; per-plan prices cross-fade on cycle change; an annual discount badge inverts when active. Designed to make the savings legible without forcing the buyer to read math.

descriptions.descriptive: Three-up plan cards with a centered pill-toggle above the grid. The toggle uses Framer Motion's layoutId so the active selection slides between Monthly and Annual with spring physics. Each plan card cross-fades the price on cycle change; an optional 'billed yearly' total fades in beneath the per-month figure when annual is selected. Featured plan lifts -3 to break the horizon line.
descriptions.usage: Use on subscription products where the annual upsell is real (15-25% off) and worth showcasing. Place mid-page after the features section. Pairs poorly with other pricing components — pick one pricing block per page so the toggle is unambiguous.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS, creator subscriptions, fintech subscriptions, productivity tools. Suits buyers who can be nudged to annual but want to confirm monthly first.

name: Pricing Single Price
description: Editorial split with long-form copy on the left and one anchored price card on the right. Designed for agency packages, single-product apps, or annual flat-rate deals where comparing tiers is theatre. The card sticks while the body scrolls past it.

descriptions.descriptive: Asymmetric 3-of-5 / 2-of-5 grid: a left-aligned editorial column with headline, body, two-up highlight bullets, and a quoted proof line; a sticky dark price card on the right with monospaced headline price, optional original-price strike-through, an inclusion checklist, primary CTA, and an optional secondary text link. Soft primary tint glow in the card corner, no neon or rainbow gradients.
descriptions.usage: Use when there is exactly one offer to sell — productised services, agency retainers, single-app one-time fees, course bundles, or founders-rate annual deals. Goes well after a long-form features section and before FAQ. Avoid placing alongside a tier-card or comparison-table block; the page should commit to one mental model.
descriptions.audienceFit: Boutique agencies, premium one-product apps, course creators, B2B services with a single flagship deliverable. Best for buyers who already know what they want and need price + scope at once.

name: Pricing Tier Cards
description: Three-up pricing tier cards with one inverted 'most popular' tier that lifts a few pixels to break the horizon line. Inclusive features use a tinted check, excluded features render with a strike-through and a minus icon — making scanability the first job.

descriptions.descriptive: Three rounded tier cards on a 3-column grid where the featured tier inverts (dark surface, light text) and translates -12px upward to anchor the eye. Prices use monospaced numerals for vertical alignment across tiers; cadence sits as a smaller suffix. Excluded features strike through instead of disappearing, so the buyer can see exactly where the upgrade boundary is.
descriptions.usage: Anchor a SaaS or productised-service site where the buying decision turns on plan comparison. Place near the bottom of the page after problem framing and feature deep-dives, then follow with FAQ to absorb objections before the final CTA. Avoid pairing with the legacy layout-pricing-tiers-01 — they are alternatives.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS, agencies with productised services, fintech subscriptions, creative tooling. Suits evaluators who scan plans before reading copy and need price + scope visible above the fold of the section.

name: Products Bundle Cards
description: Two- or three-up curated product-bundle picker with checked item lists, total price plus optional struck-through compare price, optional savings hint, and an accent badge (popular / best-value / editor-pick) on the lead card. The badged card lifts a few pixels and gets a primary-tinted border — no glow, no neon ring.

descriptions.descriptive: A grid of 2 or 3 rounded bundle cards on a light or dark slab. Each card pairs an optional 4:3 photograph with a name, short description, up to 6 checkmark items, a monospaced total price (with optional struck-through compare price and savings pill), and a per-card CTA. The badged card lifts -8px on desktop and switches its border to primary tone to communicate elevation through contrast, not effects.
descriptions.usage: Anchor a product-led home page where the buying decision turns on what's actually inside the bundle, not on plan tiers. Place after a hero or a category grid to translate browsing intent into a purchase, then follow with a dual-offer CTA or a contact section. Avoid pairing with pricing-tier-cards on the same page — the layouts are conceptually adjacent and would feel redundant.
descriptions.audienceFit: Bakeries, butcher shops, skincare boutiques, gift kits, subscription boxes, artisan goods. Suits buyers who scan what's inside before reading copy and need price + included items visible without opening a detail page.

descriptions.descriptive: Three or four large image-led category tiles arranged in a mosaic — one lead tile spans the left column on desktop while the remaining tiles stack on the right. Each tile pairs a lifestyle photograph with a category name, a small framed bestseller thumbnail, the bestseller's name, and a product-count chip in the corner. The whole tile is the click target.
descriptions.usage: Use as the primary discovery block on a homepage when the brand has a multi-category catalog and shoppers usually start by picking a department before drilling in. Reframes /products as a destination with depth rather than a flat grid. Avoid pairing with another product grid (Featured Strip, Bundle Cards) on the same page — the layouts read as redundant catalog views.
descriptions.audienceFit: Multi-category retailers, department stores, lifestyle brands with distinct collections, and marketplaces. Suits buyers who think in departments — fashion vs. home vs. accessories — before they think in individual products.

descriptions.descriptive: A sleek horizontal strip featuring curated product imagery with generous white space and bold typography. Clean lines and minimal styling create an editorial magazine feel, while energetic accent colors and friendly product photography maintain warmth without visual clutter.
descriptions.usage: Highlights selected products or collections on homepage or category pages to drive discovery and sales. Perfect for showcasing bestsellers, new arrivals, or seasonal items with clear call-to-action buttons that guide users toward purchase decisions.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to premium lifestyle brands, boutique retailers, and design-forward companies targeting discerning consumers who value quality and aesthetics. Works well for fashion, home goods, or tech brands serving style-conscious customers who appreciate curated selections.

descriptions.descriptive: An asymmetric editorial grid where one large hero tile anchors the left while four to five smaller tiles fan out across the right and bottom. Tiles read as clean photography at rest; on hover the image zooms slowly and a dark gradient surfaces the product name and price from the bottom edge. A single anchor CTA, behind a divider line, closes the composition.
descriptions.usage: Use as a homepage or category-page teaser that funnels scrollers toward the full catalog at /products. Best when the brand sells visual goods (fashion, home, lifestyle) and the catalog is small enough that a curated five or six picks can stand for the whole. Skip when the catalog is large or transactional — pair it with a Spotlight or Bundle Cards section that handles the buying decision.
descriptions.audienceFit: Premium retail, lifestyle boutiques, design-led DTC brands, and editorial commerce. Suits buyers who scan with their eyes before reading copy and trust photography to do the selling.

descriptions.descriptive: Two horizontal product rails stacked vertically and scrolling in opposite directions at slightly different speeds. Tiles read as photographs at rest; on hover the image scales gently and the product name slides up from the bottom. Both rails pause when the pointer enters the strip, giving the visitor a clean moment to scan a tile they spotted. A centered CTA with optional catalog-count framing closes the section.
descriptions.usage: Use as a homepage breadth signal — the constant motion catches peripheral vision and the size of the strip implies catalog depth without forcing a full grid. Best when the catalog has at least 12 visually distinct items and the brand can absorb a playful, energetic mood. Avoid pairing with other motion-heavy components (carousel hero, infinite-scroll testimonials) directly above or below.
descriptions.audienceFit: Lifestyle, fashion, accessories, food and beverage, and DTC brands with a strong visual identity. Suits buyers who scan with peripheral vision and respond to motion as a signal of liveliness rather than a distraction.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean, magazine-style layout with generous white space and refined typography. Features high-quality product imagery with subtle shadows and minimal text overlays. Uses sophisticated color palette with restrained visual elements that emphasize the products themselves through elegant spacing and professional presentation.
descriptions.usage: Highlights premium product collections on main pages or dedicated showcase sections. Drives conversions by presenting select items with compelling brand messaging and clear call-to-action buttons. Perfect for landing pages that need to establish product credibility while guiding users toward purchase decisions.
descriptions.audienceFit: Appeals to upscale consumers who value craftsmanship and brand prestige. Ideal for luxury goods, high-end fashion, premium electronics, or artisanal products. Resonates with established brands targeting discerning customers who associate clean design with quality and reliability.

name: Chart Stats
description: Two chart-style stats variants: an animated horizontal bar chart with count-up labels, or a single-line sparkline with a headline metric and trend pill — for sites that need comparative or longitudinal proof rather than hero numbers.

descriptions.descriptive: Variant switch between bars and sparkline. Bars: split layout with up to eight rows, each animating its fill width and counting up its label as it scrolls into view. Sparkline: a 12-point trend line that draws on view, paired with a large headline metric and a tinted up/down/flat trend pill.
descriptions.usage: Use when the page needs comparative numbers (year-over-year, multi-channel, multi-product) or a longitudinal trend (ARR over twelve months, signups over a year). Pairs naturally after a results-focused hero or before a pricing block; complements rather than replaces the count-up and KPI grid stats.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS results pages, fintech operator dashboards, marketing case-study tear-sheets, and any site whose buyers want a number with shape — not just a single dramatic figure.

name: Count-Up Stats
description: A clean, animated statistics display that reveals key numbers through count-up animations, perfect for corporate websites and professional service firms showcasing achievements, metrics, or company milestones.

descriptions.descriptive: Clean numerical displays with animated count-up effects, featuring bold typography and generous whitespace. Uses a restrained color palette with subtle accent colors to highlight key metrics. Grid-based layout maintains visual hierarchy through consistent spacing and typography scales.
descriptions.usage: Showcases quantifiable achievements, company metrics, or performance data to build credibility. Best positioned after hero sections or within about pages to reinforce brand authority through concrete numbers like years in business, clients served, or success rates.
descriptions.audienceFit: Perfect for established B2B companies, consulting firms, financial services, and professional service providers who need to demonstrate track record and reliability. Appeals to decision-makers who value data-driven proof points over emotional appeals.

name: KPI Grid
description: Dashboard-density KPI grid with monospaced numerals, trend deltas, and divider-line grouping — the comparison-first stats slot that complements the count-up hero metric panel.

descriptions.descriptive: Three-to-six metrics arranged on a divided grid (no card boxes by default) with tabular monospaced numerals, prefix/suffix support, and tinted up/down/flat trend pills next to each value. The boxed variant softens density when the section sits between visually loud neighbours.
descriptions.usage: Drop into product, results, or about pages where comparison is the point — month-over-month conversion, uptime, scale figures. Pair after a hero or before pricing; complements count-up stats rather than replacing them.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS product pages, fintech operator dashboards, agency case-study tear-sheets, and infrastructure platforms whose buyers expect numerical proof rather than emotional copy.

name: Team Culture Mosaic
description: Editorial mood-board mosaic of mixed-aspect candid lifestyle photos interleaved with 2-4 short value cards. Designed for culture / values sections — studios, agencies, restaurants, creative brands.

descriptions.descriptive: An asymmetric photo mosaic where mixed aspect ratios — portrait, square, landscape, tall — fan across a 4-column grid with row spans tuned per crop. Two to four value cards weave into the grid at deterministic positions (after the second image, then every third) so the rhythm reads woven rather than zoned. Tiles surface a small monospaced caption chip in the upper-left and a slow scale-up on hover; value cards sit in bordered surfaces with a primary-tinted ordinal numeral. Imagery should be lifestyle and candid — people working, eating together, mid-laugh — never polished press headshots.
descriptions.usage: Use on About / Culture / Careers pages where 'who we are' matters more than 'who runs us'. Place after a brand-statement hero or editorial image-text section, and pair with a stats or testimonial beat afterwards. Avoid stacking with TeamLeadershipGrid or TeamFounderSplit on the same page — both compete for the same human-ness budget and reduce the mosaic's effect.
descriptions.audienceFit: Creative agencies, design studios, restaurants and hospitality groups, lifestyle DTC brands, hospitality groups, and anywhere the team's energy is the product.

name: Founder Split
description: Editorial single-founder split. Tall 4:5 portrait beside an extended multi-paragraph bio, optional signature image, credential chips, primary and secondary CTAs. Portrait can swap sides via portraitSide.

descriptions.descriptive: A two-column editorial split that frames one person as the page. The copy column carries an eyebrow, balanced headline, an attribution block separated by a primary-tinted vertical rule, one to three bio paragraphs in a 60ch measure, and a signature row that prefers an actual signature image but falls back to a typed serif `— Name`. Optional credential chips render as monospaced uppercase pills. The portrait column is a tall 4:5 rounded image with a soft caption ribbon. Side can be flipped with `portraitSide`.
descriptions.usage: Use on About pages, founder-led service sites, and personal-brand portfolios. Place after a brand-statement hero and before social proof. Pair with TeamLeadershipGrid below to widen out from one founder to the rest of the team. Avoid pairing with another author-split layout (AuthorSplit, StatementSplit) — same rhythm twice in a row reads as filler.
descriptions.audienceFit: Founder-led professional services, solo consultancies, boutique studios, premium personal brands, executive coaches, and investor-facing leadership pages.

name: Team Hiring Callout
description: Recruiting-funnel team section. Compact 2x2 / 3x2 portrait grid with hover name/role on the left, open-roles list with department + location + per-row apply on the right, optional perks strip and bottom CTA below.

descriptions.descriptive: A two-rail recruiting section that borrows the visual language of a team grid but routes the eye toward apply links. The left rail surfaces a compact 2x2 (4 members) or 3x2 (5-6 members) thumbnail grid where name and role surface only on hover or focus — small enough that the right rail dominates the scan. The right rail is a bordered list of open roles, each row showing the title, department + location + employment-type as a monospaced meta strip, and a per-row 'Apply' chip with a corner arrow. Below the split, a bordered base-200 strip carries up to five emoji + label perks and an anchor CTA that links out to a full /careers page. A `tone` prop swaps between light page surface and an inverted dark slab.
descriptions.usage: Use on /careers landing, the lower half of an About page, or as the closing section of a Team page when 'we're hiring' is a primary funnel. Place after a stats or testimonial-video block (numbers + voices set up the apply click) and before the footer. Avoid stacking adjacent to other CTA-heavy sections (sticky CTA, image-backdrop CTA, dual-offer split) — they fight for the same click and dilute both.
descriptions.audienceFit: Growth-stage startups, agencies, professional-services firms expanding regionally, healthcare and education employers, and any company where 'come work here' deserves its own beat instead of a tucked-away link.

name: Team Journey Timeline
description: Vertical timeline of founding-story milestones with year, title, body and optional per-chapter portrait, closing in a 'Today' node card with an optional group photo. Alternating left/right rail on desktop, single left rail on mobile.

descriptions.descriptive: A chronological narrative rail where each milestone reads as year + title + body, optionally tied to a portrait of the person who joined or led that chapter. On large screens the rail alternates: years anchor one side, copy the other, joined by a 1px divider with primary-tinted dots. On mobile, the rail collapses to a single left edge with year chips above each dot. The closing 'Today' node escapes the rail format — it sits in a bordered base-200 card with a wider group photo so the timeline lands on a clear present-tense beat instead of trailing off.
descriptions.usage: Use on About / Our Story pages where the trajectory is the trust signal — founder-led brands, family businesses, agencies with strong narrative IP, second-generation businesses making a public reset. Place after a brand-statement hero and before social proof. Avoid stacking with another long-narrative content block (zigzag editorial split, case-study gallery) on the same page — the eye fatigues on a second linear read.
descriptions.audienceFit: Family businesses, founder-led brands, mid-life rebrands telling a continuity story, anniversary pages (10/15/20 years), and any segment where the journey is part of the offer.

name: Leadership Grid
description: Portrait grid for leadership pages. 4:5 portraits, name, role, optional tagline and location chip; 3- or 4-column desktop, single column on mobile, calm or framed card mood.

descriptions.descriptive: A portrait grid that prioritises faces over chrome. Each member card is a 4:5 portrait with a soft scale on hover, a small location chip in the upper-left, and a clean text block underneath: name in semibold, role in primary tint, optional one-line tagline in muted body. The grid runs 4-up on xl, 3-up on lg, 2-up on sm, and stacks below md. A `cardMood` prop swaps between borderless calm cards and framed cards on bg-base-100 for sites that want explicit grouping.
descriptions.usage: Place on About / Team pages after a brand-statement hero and before a process or values section. Works as the first social-proof beat for service businesses where the team is the product. Avoid pairing with another generic card-grid on the same page — pick one rhythm.
descriptions.audienceFit: Agencies, professional-services firms, boutique consultancies, founder-led SMBs, law and architecture practices, and any segment where the human roster is part of the pitch.

name: Team Member Spotlight
description: Featured-member spotlight with hero-sized pull quote, 4:5 portrait, name, role, optional context line, KPI credential row (years, projects, etc.) and optional CTA. Three tones: neutral, muted, inverse.

descriptions.descriptive: A single-member spotlight where the quote does the heavy lifting. The right column carries an oversized pull-quote (24-36px) with a soft serif open-quote glyph behind it, an attribution row separated by a 1px divider, and an optional KPI strip with monospaced numerals (years in role, projects shipped, satisfaction). The left column holds a tall 4:5 portrait. A `tone` prop swaps between calm neutral, soft muted surface, and inverse (dark surface, light text) for editorial impact.
descriptions.usage: Use to feature one team member with weight — a new hire announcement, a department-lead introduction, a founder spotlight when a full FounderSplit is too long. Place after a values or process section and before a CTA. Avoid stacking next to TestimonialSpotlightQuote or TeamFounderSplit — they cover the same beat.
descriptions.audienceFit: Mid-market service businesses that want to give one expert a hero moment, healthcare practices spotlighting a specialist, agencies announcing senior hires, and editorial sites running 'meet the team' columns.

name: Team Values Manifesto
description: Editorial values manifesto with eyebrow, headline + manifesto paragraph, and 3-5 numbered values rendered as alternating image-text rows. Designed for About / Culture / Careers pages where principles lead and people follow.

descriptions.descriptive: An editorial values list where each principle gets its own zigzag row: image-left/text-right alternating with text-left/image-right. The header opens with a small eyebrow rail (eyebrow + hairline + index count), then an oversized headline aligned with a short manifesto paragraph anchored to the bottom-right. Each value carries a monospaced ordinal numeral, a short hairline divider, a tight title, and a 55ch body paragraph. Photos are 4:5 portraits — people mid-work, not posed — with optional caption chips for context. A `tone` prop swaps between neutral white and a muted base-200 surface for editorial mood.
descriptions.usage: Use on About / Culture / Careers pages when the brand wants principles to lead the section rather than headshots. Place after a brand-statement hero and before a TeamLeadershipGrid, TeamFounderSplit, or stats beat — the pairing reads 'here's what we believe → here's who lives it'. Avoid stacking next to TeamMemberSpotlight; both lean editorial and compete for the same emotional weight.
descriptions.audienceFit: Mission-driven SaaS, design and creative agencies, healthcare practices, mid-market service businesses, and any brand whose principles are part of the product.

name: Infinite Scroll
description: A continuously scrolling testimonial component with bold typography and editorial spacing that creates dynamic social proof. Perfect for tech companies and agencies wanting to showcase extensive client feedback without pagination interruptions.

descriptions.descriptive: A continuous, vertically scrolling testimonial feed with bold typography and editorial-style card layouts. Features high contrast text blocks, generous whitespace, and dynamic motion that creates an energetic browsing experience while maintaining clean, professional aesthetics.
descriptions.usage: Deploy when you have numerous customer testimonials to showcase without overwhelming the page layout. Ideal for building sustained credibility through social proof, allowing users to browse testimonials at their own pace while maintaining engagement through smooth scrolling interactions.
descriptions.audienceFit: Perfect for established B2B SaaS platforms, consulting firms, and premium service providers who need to demonstrate extensive client satisfaction. Appeals to decision-makers who value comprehensive social validation and professional credibility in their vendor selection process.

name: Masonry Quotes Wall
description: Pinterest-style social-proof wall. 6-12 quote tiles laid out as a CSS-columns masonry; quote length drives vertical rhythm and every fifth tile gets an accent tone to break the column. Use when sheer volume of social proof is the message.

descriptions.descriptive: CSS-columns masonry — 1 column on mobile, 2 on tablet, 3 (or 2/4 by prop) on desktop. Each tile is a quote + author + optional context tag and company logo, with four tonal variants (muted, elevated, accent, outline) that the component auto-rotates if the agent doesn't pick one. Tiles fade-and-rise on scroll with stagger; hover lifts each tile half a step. Quote-length-driven typography keeps short quotes compact and lets long ones breathe.
descriptions.usage: Use when one strong voice isn't enough — when many short customer quotes do more social-proof work than a single hero testimonial. Place after a stats section or below a pricing block; do not stack with the spotlight or logo-ribbon testimonials on the same page (pick one carrier voice per page).
descriptions.audienceFit: B2C and prosumer SaaS with high review volume, marketplace and community products, agencies showcasing many small wins, and any segment whose proof reads better as a wall of voices than as a single editorial quote.

name: Metric Outcome Testimonial
description: Testimonial paired with a quantified KPI. Each card leads with a large count-up metric (animated on scroll) followed by a hairline rule, the customer quote, and attribution. Use when the story is 'what changed' — case studies whose proof is the number, not the brand.

descriptions.descriptive: 1-3 cards in a 1/2/3-column grid with a stagger fade-in. Each card opens with a 5xl-6xl tabular-nums metric that animates from zero on scroll, an uppercase label, an optional caption, a hairline divider, the pull-quote with serif quote glyphs, and an author footer with avatar, name, title, and optional company logo. Three tones (neutral, muted, inverse) with an editorial dark slab variant.
descriptions.usage: Use as a quantified social-proof block — case-study highlights, customer ROI tiles, or audited outcome reports. Place after a hero or below a features grid; pair upstream with a Stats KPI grid to set baseline numbers, downstream with a closing CTA. Do not stack with the Spotlight Quote or Stagger Fan testimonials.
descriptions.audienceFit: Enterprise B2B SaaS, professional services with measurable outcomes, healthcare and fintech case-study pages, agencies whose pitch is performance numbers, and any segment where proof is best expressed as a metric + a single sentence.

name: Spotlight Quote Testimonial
description: Hero-sized single testimonial — one customer's voice carries the section. Generous whitespace, large pull-quote typography, calm portrait, and a decorative serif open-quote glyph.

descriptions.descriptive: Two-column editorial layout with the pull-quote left and a 4:5 portrait right on desktop; on mobile the portrait appears as an inline avatar beside the author block. Optional eyebrow, company logo, and caption row. Three tone variants (neutral, muted, inverse) for editorial dark slabs.
descriptions.usage: Use when one customer's voice deserves a full section — a marquee enterprise win, a founder testimonial, or an editorial-style brand quote. Place after a hero or before pricing. Do not stack with other testimonial sections — pick one carrier voice per page.
descriptions.audienceFit: Premium B2B SaaS, agency portfolios, professional-services sites, and editorial-leaning brand pages whose social proof reads better as one strong voice than as a wall of cards.

name: Video Testimonial Card
description: 1-3 video testimonial tiles with poster thumbnails, hover-scale interaction, and a lightbox iframe player. The first tile gets priority width (4:5 portrait on desktop) when three are shown.

descriptions.descriptive: Tile grid with a play button, gradient legibility scrim, and an optional duration pill. Click opens a 5xl-max lightbox dialog with an autoplaying iframe (YouTube, Vimeo, or direct mp4) and a focus-trapped close control. One tile, two equal tiles, or one priority tile + two stacked secondary tiles.
descriptions.usage: Use when video testimony does work words can't — case-study walk-throughs, customer quote reels, founder retrospectives. Place after a hero or stats section; pair with a closing CTA strip or FAQ tabs at page bottom.
descriptions.audienceFit: B2B SaaS with strong customer narratives, agencies showcasing case studies, conference recap pages, and any segment whose social proof reads better in motion than as static text.
